text: Good morning, everyone.
Welcome to our presentation on "Paper2Video: Automatic Video Generation from Scientific Papers."
I'm Ziyu Zhu from the Show Lab at the National University of Singapore.
Our work introduces a novel approach to automate academic video production.
We are excited to share our findings today.
Today, I'll first discuss the motivation behind our work.
Academic presentation videos are crucial for research dissemination.
However, producing them is highly labor-intensive, involving significant effort.
The core challenge lies in the complex, multi-modal nature of video generation.
We aim to automatically generate such videos from scientific papers.
To address this, we introduce the Paper2Video benchmark.
It comprises 101 research papers, each with author-created videos and metadata.
The dataset covers diverse AI conferences like ML, CV, and NLP.
Figure 2 illustrates statistics, including paper topics, slide counts, and video lengths.
We evaluate models using Meta Similarity and PresentArena metrics.
Our proposed system is named PaperTalker, an end-to-end agent.
Figure 4 depicts the overall pipeline, from paper input to video output.
Key modules include the Slide Builder for generating LaTeX slides.
The Subtitle & Cursor Builder produces aligned subtitles and cursor movements.
This integrated approach automates the entire video creation process.
Let's delve into the Slide Builder and its layout refinement.
We generate slides directly from paper's LaTeX source.
Compiler feedback is used to iteratively fix layout and content issues.
Our Tree Search Visual Choice is a novel technique for fine-grained layout optimization.
Figure 5 visually explains this process, selecting the best slide candidate.
Next, we detail the Subtitle, Cursor, and Talker Builders.
Subtitles are generated per slide using a VLM for content alignment.
Visual Focus Prompts effectively direct the cursor to key content.
We employ UI-TARS for spatial and WhisperX for temporal alignment of the cursor.
This enhances audience guidance and accessibility significantly.
Regarding experimental settings, we compared against several baselines.
These include end-to-end video generation models and multi-agent methods.
Human-created presentations served as a direct comparison point.
Our evaluation utilized the Paper2Video benchmark dataset.
Experiments were run on NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPUs using Gemini-2.5-Flash and GPT-4.1.
Now, let's look at our main experimental results.
Table 1 summarizes the performance across various metrics.
PaperTalker significantly outperforms all baselines in automatic metrics.
Crucially, our system achieves scores comparable to HumanMade content.
The human evaluation also confirms our generated videos' high quality.
We also conducted an ablation study and analyzed efficiency.
Table 2 shows the generation cost for each method.
Cursor Highlight markedly improves content localization and QA accuracy.
Slide quality is significantly improved by our tree search refinement.
PaperTalker is 2.5 times faster than PresentAgent, highlighting efficiency.
This slide presents a visual comparison of generated videos.
Figure 8 compares PaperTalker against human-made, Veo3, and PresentAgent outputs.
PaperTalker produces rich content with accurate cursor grounding.
Unlike baselines, our videos feature an engaging talker and superior slide quality.
This visualization clearly demonstrates our approach's effectiveness.
Finally, I'll discuss limitations and outline future work.
Long-document parsing remains a challenge for extremely complex papers.
Accurately explaining highly technical equations is also difficult.
Future research includes enhanced semantic understanding for longer documents.
We also plan to develop real-time interactive video agents.
Thank you for your attention to our presentation.
We hope Paper2Video sparks further research in this exciting area.
Questions and discussion are most welcome.
Please feel free to reach out via email.
